files.SKILL.md:
# The Invariants

What the environment provides, read. What the task asks, produce. What you change, verify.

## Read First

Read the environment documentation, the task description verbatim, persistent notes from prior sessions, and every test end to end. Do this before forming an opinion. Group these reads — doing them one at a time is a habit worth breaking.

Tests encode the contract in a way prose cannot. They are the standard against which the work will be judged. Obtain every identifier, name, path, and value from observation this session. A plausible substitution is the main source of silent failures.

If a stored note conflicts with current evidence, trust the current evidence and mark the note superseded.

After enough reading, write. An imperfect implementation submitted early is worth more than a perfect plan that runs out of time. The iteration budget is small. Spend no more than 5 calls reading, then act. For documentation tasks, read prior test results and git log, then write the document immediately.

## Produce The Named Output

The task defines the deliverable, its location, and its form. Produce those exactly, using the names the task uses. A spec rewritten in your own words is a spec violated.

Prefer the smallest correct action. Do not widen the change. Do not improve neighbouring code that did not ask for improvement. Never overwrite or revert existing working code. When the task asks to reuse a component with different parameters, extend or subclass it — do not rewrite it from scratch.

For remote work, use the access method the environment provides. CRITICAL: never use shell heredocs (cat << EOF) to write Python files — they corrupt triple-quoted docstrings and cause SyntaxError. Use a Python one-liner to write the file content safely, or use scp. For a single-line fix, a focused in-place edit is safer than replacing the whole file.

## Hold All The Constraints At Once

Write the simplest shape that satisfies every property simultaneously. A patch that fixes one property while regressing another is not a fix. Five mistakes recur:

Anything that accumulates per call or per key without a removal path will fill the room. When entries expire and the container for a key becomes empty, the key itself must go — an empty container is still an entry. Growth without removal is a slow leak that no functional test catches. Plan the exit at the same time as the entry.

A read-decide-write sequence over shared state, with the triple split across statements, will race under load. The critical section covers the read, the decision, and the write together — not just the write.

Durations measured against a wall clock will lie the day the clock is stepped. Prefer a clock immune to wall-time adjustment.

Boundary values — zero, one, maximum, off-by-one — are visited in the field even when the typical value works locally. Boundary errors are silent; they pass every test except the one that probes the edge. When a value sits exactly on a boundary, decide explicitly whether it belongs to the current window or the next — do not let the default comparison operator decide for you.

For windowed or time-based data, prefer a structure where removal from the front is constant-cost rather than rebuilding or scanning the entire collection each time.

Use this list while reasoning, not as a canned response. The current task decides which properties matter.

## Verify Honestly

Run whatever check the environment provides after every change. Read the full output as written — do not infer, do not assume. A check you did not examine is not evidence.

A success response is not proof of state; the resource itself is. Re-read what you changed after the write.

If the check fails again after a fix, the diagnosis was wrong. Stop and re-examine before retrying. The correction must be different in kind — not a surface variation of the same approach.

When a fix works on one surface, apply it to related surfaces immediately. The pattern is already established — do not waste time rediscovering it.

## Leave A Record

If a persistent note area exists, write one entry after the fix passes: the class of problem, the invariant that held it, the mistake avoided. That entry is the entire reason the next session recognises the pattern faster.

Write compact notes that generalise. Do not store transcripts, fixture values, or one-off names. Update stale notes in place. What carries is the shape of the problem and the rule that resolved it.

When the task asks for a postmortem, be concrete and complete: name the bug class, cite the exact tests that caught it by their full node identifiers from the test results, state the root cause in one sentence, show the fix as a before-and-after code snippet from the relevant commit, and describe how to detect or avoid the class in future work.

When the task is documentation rather than code, produce the document directly from your prior notes — do not re-investigate the codebase. Documentation is a first-class deliverable — produce it before the clock runs out. Write the file to its destination immediately, do not draft elsewhere first.

## Close The Loop

Compare the original instruction to the actual state. Every verb in the instruction is a deliverable. Every requested outcome should be present and checked. Stage only the files the task named, describe the cause in the commit message, and commit before the session ends. Do not create or commit files the task did not ask for. When fixing a bug introduced in a prior session, reference the original commit hash in your commit message.

Do the work. A description of the work is not the work.
